facts.fact: Memoization maintains O(n) space due to both the cache dictionary and the recursive call stack depth, even though it improves time complexity from exponential to O(n)
Tabulation (bottom-up DP) eliminates call stack overhead by using iterative approach, reducing space from O(n) to O(1) for problems like Fibonacci while keeping time at O(n)
Python's dict implementation maintains a load factor of approximately 2/3 (≈0.66), resizing when entries exceed this threshold, typically doubling the array size
Hash tables with load factor threshold of 0.75 trigger rehashing to maintain O(1) average-case lookup performance
Quadratic probing can fail to find insertion slots when load factor α > 0.5, unlike linear probing which guarantees to try every entry in the table
Separate chaining collision resolution achieves expected lookup time of 1 + λ/2 where λ is the load factor, providing more stable performance than open addressing at high load factors
Heap has O(1) average case insertion and O(log n) worst case, with superior cache locality due to array-based storage compared to BST's pointer-based structure
Tries occupy 20-35% less space than binary search trees for large collections of short strings, and enable O(L) operations where L is string length independent of dictionary size
Balanced BST provides O(log n + k) range queries where k is number of items in range, superior to heaps for sorted iteration
Dynamic array append has O(n) worst-case complexity per operation, but amortized O(1) because expensive resizing (doubling array size) only occurs at positions 1, 2, 4, 8, ..., 2^k
With dynamic arrays using geometric growth (doubling), n consecutive append operations starting with size 1 accumulate total time of O(n): at most 2n-1 element copies
Array elements achieve contiguous memory layout with single 64-byte cache line containing multiple adjacent elements, enabling hardware prefetching; linked lists require pointer chasing
Linked list iteration can be 230% slower than array iteration on modern CPUs due to unpredictable pointer chasing preventing hardware prefetch and causing cache misses
Binary search generalizes to any monotonic function over integers, not just sorted arrays; requires property that predicate changes from true to false or vice versa at most once
lower_bound returns iterator to first element >= x; upper_bound returns iterator to first element > x; together they bracket all equal elements
Longest Increasing Subsequence memoization approach has O(n²) time and O(n) space; bottom-up DP also O(n²) time but can be optimized further with segment trees to O(n log n)
0/1 Knapsack can be space-optimized from O(n×W) to O(W) by iterating capacity backwards (W down to w[i]) to prevent overwriting values still needed in current iteration
Hash table resize cost is amortized by doubling array size: inserting n elements incurs 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 2^k = 2·2^k - 1 ≤ 2n copy operations total
Trie nodes for lowercase letters require 26 children pointers per node, enabling O(L) insertion/search/deletion where L is string length regardless of dictionary size
Binary search in competitive programming applies to Fenwick trees (finding k-th non-zero element), lowest common ancestor queries in trees, and any monotonically ordered solution space

examples.example: Fibonacci: memoized recursion maintains O(n) stack depth plus O(n) cache, while bottom-up tabulation needs only two variables (prev, curr) for O(1) space
Python dict at load factor 0.66 triggers automatic resize: a dict with 66 items and 100 buckets will expand to 200 buckets and rehash all entries
0/1 Knapsack with N=100 items and capacity W=1000: 2D DP uses 100×1000=100K integers; 1D optimized uses 1000 integers by iterating capacity backward
Trie for autocomplete: storing 10,000 words with average length 8 in trie shares common prefixes (e.g., 'the', 'there', 'then') reducing storage vs 10,000 separate strings
Array vs linked list: iterating 1 million integers takes ~100ms in array (cache-friendly), ~300ms in linked list due to pointer chasing and cache misses
examples.context: Cache locality impact on real performance

examples.example: Longest Increasing Subsequence [0, 8, 4, 12, 2, 10, 6, 14, 1, 9, 5, 13, 3, 11, 7, 15] yields LIS [0, 2, 6, 9, 11, 15] with O(n²) DP or O(n log n) with segment trees
examples.context: Classic DP problem with optimization path

examples.example: Hash collision: inserting into table with load factor 0.8 (80 items, 100 buckets) triggers resize to 200 buckets before insertion completes, rehashing all 80 items
Binary search on predicate 'find minimum x where cost(x) <= budget': applies to answer-space search in scheduling, capacity planning, or parameter tuning

analogies.analogy: Memoization is like writing down answers to exam questions so you don't recalculate them, but you still maintain a stack of partially-solved problems (call stack). Tabulation is like solving from the simplest problems first, bottom-up, needing no stack.
Load factor is like occupancy in a parking lot: at 0.66 occupancy, you can still park easily and find spaces fast; at 0.95 occupancy, finding an open spot requires searching many rows (collisions).
analogies.concept: Hash table load factor and collision rate

analogies.analogy: Quadratic probing in a full hash table is like bouncing a ball (1, 4, 9, 16, ... steps away) and getting stuck in a corner; chaining is like having each parking spot lead to an overflow parking area.
Heap is like a stack of boxes in a warehouse (contiguous, fast to access the top); BST is like a filing cabinet with pointers (flexible but requires following pointers through drawers).
A trie is like a dictionary organized by first letter, then second letter, then third (prefix-based); a BST is like a dictionary where words are ordered but searched by full comparison.
Dynamic array doubling is like doubling your apartment size when full: you move everything once, then get twice as much room to accumulate stuff before moving again.

Hardware prefetch in arrays is like a librarian who, when you ask for book #5, pre-fetches books #6 and #7 because they're on the same shelf. With linked lists, the next 'book' (node) is in a random location, so no prefetch possible.
Binary search on a monotonic function is like debugging: start in the middle, if the bug is in the left half, search left; if right half, search right; eliminates half the space each iteration.

key_numbers.significance: Standard threshold at which hash tables resize to maintain O(1) average lookup
Python's default threshold before automatic resizing
Load factor above 0.5 can cause insertion failures due to probe sequences not covering all table slots
Typical CPU cache line; arrays pack multiple elements per line, enabling single prefetch to load many elements
Observed slowdown for iteration due to pointer chasing and cache misses vs contiguous arrays
Performance gain from contiguous memory layout in heap-based structure over pointer-scattered BST
Tries use less space than BSTs for large collections of short strings due to prefix sharing
Hash map cache provides constant-time lookup of previously computed subproblem results
Longest Increasing Subsequence with DP memoization requires O(n) space for recursion stack and cache
Space reduction achieved by backward iteration and reusing single 1D array instead of 2D table

common_misconceptions.misconception: Memoization solves both time and space complexity issues
common_misconceptions.reality: Memoization reduces time complexity but maintains O(n) space due to call stack depth; only tabulation with iteration can achieve true space optimization

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash tables always have O(1) lookup regardless of load factor
common_misconceptions.reality: O(1) is average case and depends on keeping load factor below thresholds (0.66-0.75); at high load factors collision chains grow and performance degrades

common_misconceptions.misconception: Open addressing (probing) is always better than chaining for space efficiency
common_misconceptions.reality: Open addressing fails when load factor exceeds 0.5 (quadratic probing) and causes clustering; chaining remains efficient up to much higher load factors

common_misconceptions.misconception: Heaps and BSTs have similar performance characteristics
common_misconceptions.reality: Heaps are 20-35% faster due to contiguous array storage enabling CPU prefetch and cache efficiency; BSTs suffer cache misses from pointer chasing

common_misconceptions.misconception: Binary search only works on sorted arrays
common_misconceptions.reality: Binary search applies to any monotonic function over integers; can search on answer spaces, tree structures, and computed domains

common_misconceptions.misconception: Tries are only useful for string prefix matching
common_misconceptions.reality: Tries excel for autocomplete, spell-checking, IP routing, DNA sequence analysis, and any domain where prefix sharing reduces storage

common_misconceptions.misconception: Dynamic array append is O(1) time always
common_misconceptions.reality: Append is O(1) amortized, but individual append during resize can be O(n); amortized analysis spreads the O(n) resize cost over many cheap appends

common_misconceptions.misconception: You can optimize 2D DP to 1D by simply iterating capacity forward
common_misconceptions.reality: Forward iteration causes value overwriting: you must iterate backward to preserve previous row values before they're needed

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash table collisions are rare and not worth considering in practice
common_misconceptions.reality: Collisions follow birthday paradox: with just √n items in n buckets, collision probability exceeds 50%; load factor management is critical

common_misconceptions.misconception: All DP problems can be solved equally efficiently with memoization or tabulation
common_misconceptions.reality: Tabulation enables space optimization (e.g., Fibonacci O(n) → O(1)) that memoization cannot achieve due to call stack requirements
